title: Heart Failure — Chronic (HFrEF)
oneLiner: HF with LVEF <40%; common, progressive, 50% 5-year mortality; early diagnosis & guideline-directed therapy improve outcomes.

sections.Chronic Heart Failure (HFrEF): **One-liner:** HF with LVEF <40%; common, progressive, 50% 5-year mortality; early diagnosis & guideline-directed therapy improve outcomes.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute pulmonary oedema** — *frothy pink sputum + bilateral crackles up to apices* -> requires immediate CPAP/NIV + IV furosemide + nitrates
- **Cardiogenic shock** — *cold, clammy, SBP <90 mmHg + narrow pulse pressure + raised JVP* -> needs inotropes +/- IV fluids cautiously, early cardiology/critical care
- **Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF)** — *pulseless / broad-complex tachycardia* -> immediate defibrillation per ALS algorithm
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Progressive exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea (PND), peripheral oedema (ankle/sacral), fatigue
- **Key exam findings:** Raised JVP + hepatojugular reflux, displaced apex beat (LV enlargement), S3 gallop, bilateral inspiratory crackles, pitting oedema, hepatomegaly
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - COPD exacerbation – *smoker, productive cough, hyperresonance, BNP normal*
  - Pneumonia – *fever, focal crackles, raised CRP, no fluid overload*
  - Chronic renal failure – *uraemic symptoms, fluid overload but no cardiac enlargement, normal BNP*
  - Cirrhosis/ascites – *abdominal distension, spider naevi, no breathlessness without ascites*
  - Anaemia – *pale, low Hb, no elevated JVP*
- **Splitter:** Elevated BNP/NT-proBNP + echocardiogram (LVEF <40%) = HFrEF; normal BNP + normal echo = alternative diagnosis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** ECG (sinus tachycardia, Q waves, LBBB, AF), pulse oximetry, lying and standing blood pressure, urine output chart, daily weight
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia), U&E (renal function, K+), LFT (congestive hepatopathy), TFT (thyroid causes), BNP/NT-proBNP (elevated), troponin (if acute), iron studies (iron deficiency common)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Chest X-ray (cardiomegaly, upper lobe diversion, Kerley B lines, pleural effusion)
  - **Second-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram (LVEF, wall motion, valve disease, filling pressures)
- **Special tests:** Cardiac MRI (if echo inadequate or to assess iron overload), stress echo (if ischaemic suspect), coronary angiography (if angina / acute coronary syndrome)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** ESC Heart Failure criteria: symptoms + signs + LVEF <40% on echo; NT-proBNP >125 pg/mL (if chronic) supports
- **Never-miss test:** Echocardiogram – omission delays life‑saving therapy and allows preventable progression
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  If acute decompensation:  
  – Sit patient upright, oxygen to SpO₂ 92-96% (if hypoxic)  
  – IV furosemide 40-80 mg (higher if already on loop diuretic)  
  – SL/IV nitrate (glyceryl trinitrate) if SBP >110 mmHg  
  – CPAP/NIV if respiratory distress ± pCO₂ >6 kPa  
  – Do NOT start beta‑blocker acutely if in decompensation
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Daily weight, strict fluid balance (aim -500 mL to -1 L/day)  
  – Titrate loop diuretic to euvolaemia (furosemide IV 40-120 mg/day, consider bumetanide if resistant)  
  – Start/restart oral therapy once stable (below)  
  – Check U&E daily during diuresis and after initiating ACEi/MRA  
  – Consider iron IV (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin <100 µg/L and TSAT <20%  
  – Involve heart failure specialist nurse / cardiology
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Guideline‑directed medical therapy (GDMT) – see Drugs below  
  – Device therapy: CRT (if LBBB + LVEF ≤35% on optimal therapy), ICD (secondary prevention or primary if persistent LVEF ≤35%)  
  – Multidisciplinary follow-up within 2 weeks of discharge  
  – Lifestyle: salt restriction (<2g Na/day), daily weighing, smoking cessation, exercise rehabilitation
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: restrict to 1.5-2 L/day if fluid overload; no routine IV fluids unless hypotensive  
  – VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if eGFR >30), unless major bleed risk  
  – Diet: low sodium (2 g/day) if hypertensive/oedematous; no NBM unless acute decompensation  
  – Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (stable) or 1-hourly if decompensated; daily weight
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (initiate sequentially):**
  - **ACE inhibitor** (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg od) – up‑titrate as tolerated to 5 mg bd or 10 mg od; monitor K+, Cr at each dose change
  - **Beta‑blocker** (e.g., bisoprolol 1.25 mg od) – start once euvolaemic, up‑titrate every 2-4 weeks to 10 mg od; use only in stable HF (not acute oedema)
  - **Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist** (MRA) – spironolactone 25 mg od (or eplerenone 25 mg bd if gynaecomastia); check K+ before starting, avoid if Cr >221 µmol/L or K+ >5 mmol/L
  - **SGLT2 inhibitor** – dapagliflozin 10 mg od or empagliflozin 10 mg od; reduces HF hospitalisation and CV death
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **ARB** (e.g., candesartan 4-8 mg od) if ACEi‑intolerant (cough, angioedema)
  - **Hydralazine + nitrate** (e.g., isosorbide dinitrate 20-40 mg tds) if African‑Caribbean heritage or intolerant to both ACEi/ARB
  - **Loop diuretic** (furosemide 40–120 mg od/bd, or bumetanide 1–5 mg od) – adjust to euvolaemia; no mortality benefit, only symptom relief
  - **Digoxin** (62.5-125 µg od) – if AF with rapid ventricular rate despite beta‑blocker or if NYHA III-IV on optimal therapy
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - ACEi + MRA + K+ supplements → severe hyperkalaemia
  - SGLT2i → euglycaemic DKA (rare), genital mycotic infections, volume depletion
  - Beta‑blocker + verapamil → bradycardia, heart block
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACEi/ARB reduce dose if Cr rises ≥30% or K+ >5.5; stop if Cr >354 µmol/L
  - SGLT2i – avoid if eGFR <20 mL/min (dapagliflozin) or <30 (empagliflozin); initiate only if eGFR ≥20‑30
  - Spironolactone – avoid if Cr >221 µmol/L or K+ >5.0
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – **Overdiuresis (furosemide IV high dose)** → hypovolaemia, AKI, hypotension → stop or reduce; always hold beta‑blocker if hypotensive  
  – **Stopping beta‑blocker abruptly** → rebound tachycardia, increased mortality (taper over 2 weeks if needed)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Pulmonary oedema, acute kidney injury (prerenal), hyperkalaemia (ACEi/MRA), hypotension, arrhythmias (AF, VT), sudden cardiac death
- **Chronic:** Worsening renal failure, hyponatraemia (dilutional), cardiac cachexia, iron deficiency anaemia, refractory oedema (needs combination diuretics), depression
sections.Prognosis: - 50% mortality at 5 years; median survival ~2 years if NYHA IV. Early GDMT reduces mortality by ~30-50%. Prognosis worse if LVEF ≤25%, low sodium, high BNP, frequent admissions.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Daily standing blood pressure** before beta‑blocker/ACEi titration – a drop >20 mmHg systolic suggests volume depletion or vasodilation; hold and reassess.
- **Check weight every morning** – gain >1 kg/overnight or >2 kg/week indicates fluid overload; increase diuretic.
- **Start beta‑blocker on the same day as diuretic if patient is euvolaemic and SBP >100 mmHg** – recent trials (STRONG-HF) support rapid up‑titration.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Starting a beta‑blocker during acute pulmonary oedema** – worsens decompensation; wait until stable and no longer requiring IV diuretics.
- **Stopping diuretics before achieving euvolaemia** – leads to re‑admission; continue until JVP normal, crackles clear, oedema resolved.
- **Ignoring iron deficiency** – iron deficiency (ferritin <100 or TSAT <20%) occurs in ~50% of HFrEF and worsens symptoms; treat with IV iron even if not anaemic.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  – Worsening shortness of breath (can’t walk room length)  
  – Weight gain >2 kg over 1-2 days despite increased diuretic  
  – Dizziness / fainting (hypotension) or palpitations  
  – Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea (dehydration→AKI)
- **Follow-up:** Heart failure clinic within 2 weeks of discharge; GP review at 1 week for U&E and clinical check. Echocardiogram at 3-6 months to reassess LVEF after optimal therapy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / cardiology / critical care if:**  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 (despite treatment)  
  – Lactate >2 mmol/L (suggesting hypoperfusion)  
  – GCS <15 or new confusion  
  – Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for >2 hours  
  – SBP <90 mmHg with signs of organ hypoperfusion (oliguria, drowsy, pale)  
  – New atrial fibrillation with fast rate >150 bpm or unstable VT

rawMarkdown:
## Chronic Heart Failure (HFrEF)
**One-liner:** HF with LVEF <40%; common, progressive, 50% 5-year mortality; early diagnosis & guideline-directed therapy improve outcomes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute pulmonary oedema** — *frothy pink sputum + bilateral crackles up to apices* -> requires immediate CPAP/NIV + IV furosemide + nitrates
- **Cardiogenic shock** — *cold, clammy, SBP <90 mmHg + narrow pulse pressure + raised JVP* -> needs inotropes +/- IV fluids cautiously, early cardiology/critical care
- **Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF)** — *pulseless / broad-complex tachycardia* -> immediate defibrillation per ALS algorithm

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Progressive exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea (PND), peripheral oedema (ankle/sacral), fatigue
- **Key exam findings:** Raised JVP + hepatojugular reflux, displaced apex beat (LV enlargement), S3 gallop, bilateral inspiratory crackles, pitting oedema, hepatomegaly

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - COPD exacerbation – *smoker, productive cough, hyperresonance, BNP normal*
  - Pneumonia – *fever, focal crackles, raised CRP, no fluid overload*
  - Chronic renal failure – *uraemic symptoms, fluid overload but no cardiac enlargement, normal BNP*
  - Cirrhosis/ascites – *abdominal distension, spider naevi, no breathlessness without ascites*
  - Anaemia – *pale, low Hb, no elevated JVP*
- **Splitter:** Elevated BNP/NT-proBNP + echocardiogram (LVEF <40%) = HFrEF; normal BNP + normal echo = alternative diagnosis

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** ECG (sinus tachycardia, Q waves, LBBB, AF), pulse oximetry, lying and standing blood pressure, urine output chart, daily weight
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia), U&E (renal function, K+), LFT (congestive hepatopathy), TFT (thyroid causes), BNP/NT-proBNP (elevated), troponin (if acute), iron studies (iron deficiency common)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Chest X-ray (cardiomegaly, upper lobe diversion, Kerley B lines, pleural effusion)
  - **Second-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram (LVEF, wall motion, valve disease, filling pressures)
- **Special tests:** Cardiac MRI (if echo inadequate or to assess iron overload), stress echo (if ischaemic suspect), coronary angiography (if angina / acute coronary syndrome)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** ESC Heart Failure criteria: symptoms + signs + LVEF <40% on echo; NT-proBNP >125 pg/mL (if chronic) supports
- **Never-miss test:** Echocardiogram – omission delays life‑saving therapy and allows preventable progression

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  If acute decompensation:  
  – Sit patient upright, oxygen to SpO₂ 92-96% (if hypoxic)  
  – IV furosemide 40-80 mg (higher if already on loop diuretic)  
  – SL/IV nitrate (glyceryl trinitrate) if SBP >110 mmHg  
  – CPAP/NIV if respiratory distress ± pCO₂ >6 kPa  
  – Do NOT start beta‑blocker acutely if in decompensation
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Daily weight, strict fluid balance (aim -500 mL to -1 L/day)  
  – Titrate loop diuretic to euvolaemia (furosemide IV 40-120 mg/day, consider bumetanide if resistant)  
  – Start/restart oral therapy once stable (below)  
  – Check U&E daily during diuresis and after initiating ACEi/MRA  
  – Consider iron IV (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin <100 µg/L and TSAT <20%  
  – Involve heart failure specialist nurse / cardiology
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Guideline‑directed medical therapy (GDMT) – see Drugs below  
  – Device therapy: CRT (if LBBB + LVEF ≤35% on optimal therapy), ICD (secondary prevention or primary if persistent LVEF ≤35%)  
  – Multidisciplinary follow-up within 2 weeks of discharge  
  – Lifestyle: salt restriction (<2g Na/day), daily weighing, smoking cessation, exercise rehabilitation
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: restrict to 1.5-2 L/day if fluid overload; no routine IV fluids unless hypotensive  
  – VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if eGFR >30), unless major bleed risk  
  – Diet: low sodium (2 g/day) if hypertensive/oedematous; no NBM unless acute decompensation  
  – Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (stable) or 1-hourly if decompensated; daily weight

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (initiate sequentially):**
  - **ACE inhibitor** (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg od) – up‑titrate as tolerated to 5 mg bd or 10 mg od; monitor K+, Cr at each dose change
  - **Beta‑blocker** (e.g., bisoprolol 1.25 mg od) – start once euvolaemic, up‑titrate every 2-4 weeks to 10 mg od; use only in stable HF (not acute oedema)
  - **Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist** (MRA) – spironolactone 25 mg od (or eplerenone 25 mg bd if gynaecomastia); check K+ before starting, avoid if Cr >221 µmol/L or K+ >5 mmol/L
  - **SGLT2 inhibitor** – dapagliflozin 10 mg od or empagliflozin 10 mg od; reduces HF hospitalisation and CV death
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **ARB** (e.g., candesartan 4-8 mg od) if ACEi‑intolerant (cough, angioedema)
  - **Hydralazine + nitrate** (e.g., isosorbide dinitrate 20-40 mg tds) if African‑Caribbean heritage or intolerant to both ACEi/ARB
  - **Loop diuretic** (furosemide 40–120 mg od/bd, or bumetanide 1–5 mg od) – adjust to euvolaemia; no mortality benefit, only symptom relief
  - **Digoxin** (62.5-125 µg od) – if AF with rapid ventricular rate despite beta‑blocker or if NYHA III-IV on optimal therapy
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - ACEi + MRA + K+ supplements → severe hyperkalaemia
  - SGLT2i → euglycaemic DKA (rare), genital mycotic infections, volume depletion
  - Beta‑blocker + verapamil → bradycardia, heart block
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACEi/ARB reduce dose if Cr rises ≥30% or K+ >5.5; stop if Cr >354 µmol/L
  - SGLT2i – avoid if eGFR <20 mL/min (dapagliflozin) or <30 (empagliflozin); initiate only if eGFR ≥20‑30
  - Spironolactone – avoid if Cr >221 µmol/L or K+ >5.0
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – **Overdiuresis (furosemide IV high dose)** → hypovolaemia, AKI, hypotension → stop or reduce; always hold beta‑blocker if hypotensive  
  – **Stopping beta‑blocker abruptly** → rebound tachycardia, increased mortality (taper over 2 weeks if needed)

### Complications
- **Acute:** Pulmonary oedema, acute kidney injury (prerenal), hyperkalaemia (ACEi/MRA), hypotension, arrhythmias (AF, VT), sudden cardiac death
- **Chronic:** Worsening renal failure, hyponatraemia (dilutional), cardiac cachexia, iron deficiency anaemia, refractory oedema (needs combination diuretics), depression

### Prognosis
- 50% mortality at 5 years; median survival ~2 years if NYHA IV. Early GDMT reduces mortality by ~30-50%. Prognosis worse if LVEF ≤25%, low sodium, high BNP, frequent admissions.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Daily standing blood pressure** before beta‑blocker/ACEi titration – a drop >20 mmHg systolic suggests volume depletion or vasodilation; hold and reassess.
- **Check weight every morning** – gain >1 kg/overnight or >2 kg/week indicates fluid overload; increase diuretic.
- **Start beta‑blocker on the same day as diuretic if patient is euvolaemic and SBP >100 mmHg** – recent trials (STRONG-HF) support rapid up‑titration.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Starting a beta‑blocker during acute pulmonary oedema** – worsens decompensation; wait until stable and no longer requiring IV diuretics.
- **Stopping diuretics before achieving euvolaemia** – leads to re‑admission; continue until JVP normal, crackles clear, oedema resolved.
- **Ignoring iron deficiency** – iron deficiency (ferritin <100 or TSAT <20%) occurs in ~50% of HFrEF and worsens symptoms; treat with IV iron even if not anaemic.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  – Worsening shortness of breath (can’t walk room length)  
  – Weight gain >2 kg over 1-2 days despite increased diuretic  
  – Dizziness / fainting (hypotension) or palpitations  
  – Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea (dehydration→AKI)
- **Follow-up:** Heart failure clinic within 2 weeks of discharge; GP review at 1 week for U&E and clinical check. Echocardiogram at 3-6 months to reassess LVEF after optimal therapy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / cardiology / critical care if:**  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 (despite treatment)  
  – Lactate >2 mmol/L (suggesting hypoperfusion)  
  – GCS <15 or new confusion  
  – Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for >2 hours  
  – SBP <90 mmHg with signs of organ hypoperfusion (oliguria, drowsy, pale)  
  – New atrial fibrillation with fast rate >150 bpm or unstable VT
